summary: Legacy validated record without machine-checkable validation proof. Policy-backed control for the password reveal button in Windows credential entry surfaces. Turning it off slightly reduces shoulder-surfing convenience risks, but users lose the built-in way to briefly reveal what they typed.

setting.name: Password Reveal Button
setting.casual_explanation: Windows can show an eye button next to password boxes so people can quickly check what they typed. This policy decides whether that button appears.
setting.why_people_change_it: People disable it when they want fewer password-reveal affordances on shared or security-sensitive machines. They leave it enabled when they want easier password entry and fewer typing mistakes.
setting.tradeoffs_overview: Turning it off removes a convenience feature that helps catch typing mistakes, but it also removes a quick way to reveal a password on screen.
setting.professional_notes: CredUI.admx documents DisablePasswordReveal under HKLM or HKCU Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\CredUI with enabledValue 1 and disabledValue 0. The local ADML help text says enabled hides the reveal button, while disabled or not configured leaves it visible.

allowed_values.label: Not configured
allowed_values.meaning: Windows keeps the normal password reveal button behavior.

allowed_values.label: Password reveal allowed
allowed_values.meaning: The policy is disabled and the password reveal button can be shown.

allowed_values.label: Password reveal disabled
allowed_values.meaning: The password reveal button is not displayed in supported password entry experiences.

app_current_implementation.notes: The current app writes DisablePasswordReveal = 1 under the machine policy path, which matches the documented enabled policy state for hiding the button.

windows_defaults.label: Windows policy default
windows_defaults.applies_to: Windows systems supporting the CredUI password reveal policy

states.rationale: If the policy is not configured, Windows keeps the normal reveal-button behavior.

windows_defaults.notes: The policy can exist under user or machine scope. This record tracks the machine-scoped implementation used by the app.

recommended_profiles.label: Windows default
intended_for: People who want easier password entry
avoid_for: Shared or higher-sensitivity machines that want fewer reveal affordances
recommended_profiles.good_when: You want the usual Windows password entry convenience.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You want to remove the reveal button everywhere this policy applies.
tradeoffs: Users can more easily check typing mistakes.

recommended_profiles.label: Hide password reveal button
avoid_for: Users who rely on the reveal button to reduce typing mistakes

states.rationale: Hide the password reveal button through policy.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You want fewer ways for passwords to be shown on screen.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: Users regularly need visual confirmation while typing passwords.
tradeoffs: Password entry can be less convenient for some users.

evidence.title: Local Microsoft CredUI.admx mapping
evidence.summary: The local ADMX file maps DisablePasswordReveal to Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\CredUI\DisablePasswordReveal with enabledValue 1 and disabledValue 0.

evidence.title: Local Microsoft CredUI.adml help text
evidence.summary: The local ADML help text says enabling the policy hides the password reveal button, while disabling or not configuring it leaves the button visible.

evidence.title: Current app implementation
evidence.summary: The provider writes DisablePasswordReveal = 1 under the machine policy path.

decision.why: The local Microsoft CredUI policy files explicitly define the policy path Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\CredUI, the value name DisablePasswordReveal, and the enabled/disabled mapping. The app writes the documented enabled-state value 1 on that exact policy surface.

validation_proof.exact_quote_or_path: <policy name="DisablePasswordReveal" class="Both" ... key="Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\CredUI" valueName="DisablePasswordReveal"> <enabledValue><decimal value="1" /></enabledValue> <disabledValue><decimal value="0" /></disabledValue>; CredUI.adml: "If you enable this policy setting, the password reveal button will not be displayed..."
validation_proof.notes: The local official Microsoft ADMX file defines the exact policy path and value name used by the app, with enabledValue 1 and disabledValue 0. The paired ADML help text explains that enabling the policy hides the password reveal button.
